title: IRS Code 700 Meaning (Interest Assessed) – For Tax Professionals
description: IRS Code 700 means interest was assessed on the module. Compare dates and amounts with codes 150, 290, and 570, then document it in a client-ready report.
content:
IRS Transcript Codes • Built for 750,000+ U.S. Tax Professionals

IRS Code 700 Meaning

IRS Code 700 means interest was assessed on the module. This page explains when it shows up, what it usually signals, and how to summarize it in plain English for clients. Want to see what clients get? Open the sample report, then run your own transcript when you’re ready.

Try the parser (requires credits)

What is IRS Code 700?

Plain-English definition: IRS Code 700 means the IRS assessed interest on the tax module. Interest is typically tied to time, balance due, and/or adjustments that change what was owed for a period.

Common transcript type
Account Transcript
Usually appears near
Codes 150, 290, 570
Typical context
Balance due over time, adjustments, or delayed resolution
Client-friendly summary
"Interest was added to the account"

When does Code 700 appear?

  • After an assessment: Often posts after a tax is assessed (for example, after Code 150) and a balance due exists across time.
  • After an adjustment: May post after an additional assessment or change (for example, after Code 290) that increases what was owed for a past period.
  • When timing matters: Can appear when resolution is delayed (holds, missing info, or slow processing), extending the period interest accrues.

What does Code 700 usually signal?

If this appears with Code 150

Interest is being computed against an assessed module. The next question is whether the module stayed unpaid long enough to accrue interest.

If this appears with Code 290

An adjustment increased what was owed, and interest may be posting due to the effective period the amount relates to. Reconcile the sequence using dates and amounts.

If this appears with Code 570

A hold can delay resolution. Even if the taxpayer expects a refund, interest can still post in certain balance scenarios. The module balance and later releases are what matter.

What should a tax professional do next?

Run a quick, consistent checklist. Keep it boring. Boring equals billable.

  1. Amount: Document the interest amount and whether it’s increasing the balance due.
  2. Balance: Confirm the current module balance and whether credits/payments offset it.
  3. Dates: Compare posting dates across assessment/adjustment/hold codes to explain why interest accrued.
  4. Source event: Identify the likely trigger (assessment, adjustment, delay) that created the interest window.

Example: how this looks in a parsed report

Below is a simplified view of how Code 700 can appear in a client-ready transcript report with context:

Transcript Interest Timeline (Simplified)
Code 150 – Return filed and assessed
Date: 04/15/2024
Code 700 – Interest assessed
Amount: $143 • Date: 06/03/2024
Code 290 – Additional tax assessed (adjustment)
Note: Use dates/amounts to tie interest to the adjustment window

FAQ

What does IRS Code 700 mean on a tax transcript?

IRS Code 700 means interest was assessed on the module. Confirm the posting date and amount, then reconcile it against the module balance and nearby assessment/adjustment codes.

Is IRS Code 700 the same as a penalty?

No. Code 700 is interest assessed. Penalties are typically reflected by different transaction codes.

Why would Code 700 appear after Code 150 or 290?

Code 150 establishes the assessed tax, and Code 290 can indicate an adjustment. Interest (700) can post when there is a balance due over time or when an adjustment changes what was owed for a period.
